text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Tomas 
Consultant Neurologist   
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Tomas 
Consultant Neurologist ,  

Michael Johnson, DOB: 22-11-1974, Hospital No: M458721 NHS No. 8932456710  
45 Elmwood Avenue, London, SW3 4RT

Thank you for asking me to review this patient in the neurology clinic. I met with him today accompanied by his partner. He reports a longstanding history of episodic events for which prior records are limited. Social alcohol use is acknowledged and he states he has been actively reducing intake over recent months. He volunteered that the more disruptive episodes have tended to cluster towards weekends when he previously drank more, and he is trialling further reduction. 

He describes two main patterns of events witnessed primarily by his partner. The first involves a sudden pause, vacant expression, and head turning to the right, followed by stiffening and irregular shaking of all limbs lasting approximately 90–120 seconds, with a confused period afterwards of 20–40 minutes. On two occasions there has been tongue-biting; no clear incontinence was reported. These have occurred episodically, and he notes that bouts have been more likely on evenings following social gatherings with alcohol, although this relationship is not fully consistent. He now refers to these as his “big episodes”. Over the past six months he estimates approximately one such episode every three to four weeks, with a greater likelihood on nights when he has had several drinks the same day. He is actively tapering alcohol and logs his consumption on a diary app. He and his partner also note that sleep deprivation appears to lower the threshold for events, particularly after late nights out. 

The second pattern consists of brief spells of pallor, lip-smacking, and loss of awareness for less than a minute, after which he is mildly disoriented for a few minutes. These have occurred more sporadically and without a clear trigger. He denies preceding aura. No clear morning predominance is reported. 

There is no history of significant head injury, meningitis, or febrile convulsions. Family history is non-contributory. He is employed as a graphic designer and does not drive. Medications currently include levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily and vitamin D; adherence is reported as good. He previously tried lamotrigine but discontinued due to rash. He does not use recreational drugs. Social alcohol use is ongoing but he has reduced to 6–8 units per week from a prior estimate of 14–18 units; he is aiming to lower further. 

Examination today was unremarkable with normal speech, cranial nerves, tone, power, coordination and gait. No focal neurological signs were elicited. Blood pressure 128/78 mmHg, pulse 72 bpm regular. 

Prior investigations available to me include a routine EEG from 2023 described as normal and an MRI brain (1.5T) from 2022 reported as no focal structural lesion. I have not yet seen the raw imaging. Given the ongoing events and the pattern described—particularly the increased likelihood of episodes on evenings associated with social drinking—I have advised further characterisation. I have arranged:
- Prolonged ambulatory EEG with video capture if feasible.
- Repeat MRI brain with epilepsy protocol (3T) for higher resolution review.
- Bloods including FBC, U&E, LFTs, bone profile, B12/folate, TFTs, and vitamin D.

We discussed safety measures extensively, including bathing and kitchen precautions, sleep regularity, and logging of events. He will continue his current medication while we obtain further data. He is willing to persist with alcohol reduction; I have asked him to maintain a detailed diary correlating events with sleep, stress, and any alcohol consumed. He understands DVLA guidance as it pertains to his current situation. 

Plan:
1) Arrange ambulatory EEG with video and repeat MRI brain (3T) epilepsy protocol.  
2) Continue levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily pending results.  
3) Event and lifestyle diary to note timing, sleep, stressors, and any alcohol intake; he will bring this to the next review.  
4) Routine bloods as above.  
5) Follow-up in clinic in 10–12 weeks, or earlier if there are clusters, injuries, or any prolonged recovery. 

In summary, he describes recurrent events with a tendency to occur on evenings after social drinking, though not exclusively so, and he is currently reducing intake. Further investigation is warranted to clarify the phenotype and guide management. The consultation was calm and cooperative overall; mood neutral, no psychotic features, and cognition intact. 

Please let me know if I can provide any further information. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Over the past six months he estimates approximately one such episode every three to four weeks

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes two event types. The first (“big episodes”) has an explicit frequency: “over the past six months… approximately one such episode every three to four weeks.” The second (“brief spells”) is described as occurring “more sporadically” without a numeric rate. Per the rules, prioritize explicit numeric expressions and select the highest frequency. The second type lacks a quantifiable frequency and is described as more sporadic, so the highest known frequency is the big episodes at 1 per 3 to 4 weeks. The patient is not seizure free.
seizure_frequency_number: Over the past six months he estimates approximately one such episode every three to four weeks

flaws.description: Gold (1 per 3 to 4 weeks) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
